title: Revenue Recognition
description:
Premium Analysis Guide: AS vs Ind AS 115 vs IFRS 15 vs US GAAP ASC
            606

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION A: Core Technical Comparison Table
tableData: Revenue recognized when risks and rewards are transferred and income is earned and realizable.
Revenue recognized when control of goods or services transfers to the customer, based on a five-step model.
Same five-step control-based model as Ind AS; principle-based.
Same five-step model as IFRS but with more detailed US guidance.
From "earned/realized" and risks & rewards to control and performance obligations.
Limited explicit focus on "contract with customer"; emphasis on individual transactions and invoicing.
Explicit definition of contract with customer with enforceable rights and obligations.
Same as Ind AS; strong contract-based framework.
Same as IFRS; legal contract and enforceable rights drive analysis.
Shift from invoice driven to contract driven analysis.
Revenue when significant risks and rewards are transferred, collection certain. Usually at invoice/contract price, adjusted for known rebates.
Identify contract and performance obligations, recognize revenue when/as obligations are satisfied. Measure transaction price, estimate variables.
Same model; revenue over time or at a point in time based on control transfer. Same transaction price model.
Same model and criteria; more detailed application guidance. Explicit treatment of variables & financing.
From a single event (delivery) to a pattern of performance. From "billed value" to transaction price estimation.
Limited concept of subsequent re-measurement except for provisions/discounts; revenue rarely revisited.
Adjust revenue prospectively if estimates (variable consideration, progress, etc.) change.
Same; high reliance on updates of estimates.
Same; strong use of updated estimates and cumulative catch-up adjustments.
From static to dynamic measurement linked to updated estimates.
Over time vs point in time
No formal over-time model; long-term contracts mainly via AS 7/AS 9 with completion method or POC in limited cases.
Distinct criteria to determine over time vs point in time; cost-to-cost or output methods to measure progress.
Same; extensive guidance for construction/long-term arrangements.
Introduces systematic over-time recognition linked to performance progress.
Sparse guidance; often split by legal contract/milestone; risk of arbitrary allocation.
Identify distinct performance obligations and allocate transaction price based on standalone selling prices.
Same; lots of industry examples (software, telecom, etc.).
From ad-hoc allocation to principled allocation based on standalone prices.
Limited and conservative; often recognized only when no significant uncertainty.
Estimate variable consideration (discounts, penalties) and apply constraint to avoid significant revenue reversal.
Same; specific guidance for royalties, sales-or usage-based fees.
Introduces probability-weighted / expected value thinking with explicit constraint.
Advances and unbilled revenue shown, but not under a unified conceptual framework.
Explicit contract assets (unbilled rights) and contract liabilities (deferred revenue).
Same; terminology similar ("contract asset" and "contract liability").
From generic advances/unbilled to structured contract positions.
Limited, less developed guidance; focus on legal form and risk.
Control-based principal vs agent assessment (gross vs net revenue).
From legal/title focus to control and exposure analysis.
Not systematically addressed; interest element often not separated unless explicit.
Identify and adjust for significant financing component in transaction price when cash timing diverges.
Same; interest income/expense split is explicit.
From ignoring time value to explicit financing vs revenue split.
Measure at fair value and include in transaction price.
Same; measure at fair value, or indirectly by reference to standalone selling price.
From "billed cash only" mindset to full economic consideration.
Treated as revision of contract terms with limited principle-level guidance.
Distinct rules for separate contracts vs modifications, depending on scope/price.
From ad-hoc to structured modification accounting.
Options for full retrospective or modified retrospective adoption.
Similar full or modified retrospective transition.
Limited requirements, high-level splits by broad categories only.
Extensive narrative. Disaggregation by type, geography, market, timing, and contract balances.
Similar depth and themes as Ind AS. Same disaggregation requirements.
Equally comprehensive; SEC overlays add more granularity.
From minimal to multi-dimensional revenue analytics in notes.
Substantially converged with IFRS 15, with limited Indian carve-outs.
Not word-for-word converged, but outcome converged in most contracts.
Global move to a unified revenue logic.
Year 1Rs. 90 Cr revenue (60% of Rs. 150 Cr)

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION D: Side-by-Side Impact Table (Project Only)
SECTION F: Balance Sheet Impact - End of Year 1
tableData: Earlier profit recognition under new standards
AS 9 shows higher asset base due to WIP capitalisation without profit
Net operating cash flow from project
Total cash drain is identical across all frameworks
Higher growth reported mechanically due to new rules
Absolute earnings per share sees one-time surge

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION I: Earnings Quality - Signals Table
tableData: Sharp jump in revenue in transition year vs AS
Over-time contracts now recognized using cost-to-cost model; earlier AS deferred revenue.
Earnings are front-loaded, not necessarily "better"; economics unchanged.
Normalize by spreading project revenue over life.
Higher contract assets replacing inventory and advances
Same project WIP now presented as contract asset instead of inventory/advance.
No real change in risk; just reflects enforceable right to payment.
Treat as working capital, adjust WC metrics.
Increase in disclosure volume on revenue
New standards require detailed contract and performance obligation disclosures.
More transparency → higher earnings quality if consistent.
Read revenue note like a mini-MD&A.
Margin stability despite revenue timing change
Revenue moved forward but margin % remains similar across project life.
Underlying project economics are stable; only timing shifted.
Focus on lifetime contract, not single-year spikes.
Greater judgment disclosures on variable consideration
Management now explicitly estimates and constrains variable consideration.
Earnings more judgment-sensitive; scope for optimism/conservatism.
Adjust EBIT to align with milestones.
Combine contract assets and trade receivables.
Asset days look high vs Inv.
Create pro forma EPS using old logic.
Rebuild revenue or remove first-year impact of new over-time contracts.
Underlying growth closer to high single-digits.
Examine revenue note to see if margin expansion is pure timing.
Margin quality unchanged; expansion is standard-driven.
Adjust denominator to exclude contract assets for WIP.
Operational efficiency may not have improved.
Build normalized EPS excluding standard-driven timing.
True earnings power between old and new metrics.
Largely timing: same contracts, recognized over time now. Growth moved from X% to Y%.
Same total project profit; cash flows unchanged.
Lifetime margins unchanged; only period allocation changed. Margin moved from X% to Y%.
No - represent enforceable rights to payment.
"Are IFRS and US GAAP outcomes different?"
For these contracts, Ind AS, IFRS and ASC 606 are identical.
Rebuild normalized earnings and re-test value.
Read transition note, focus on reported EPS.
Increase weight if they trust management.
Ask for pre/post-transition bridges, model normalized.
Adjust for timing; ensure covenant headroom valid.
Emphasising "strong rev growth" without explaining standard
🟡Yellow flag - demand bridge to old basis.
Likely higher earnings quality; fewer hidden issues.
Avoiding disclosure of contract assets breakdown
May be hiding loss-making or delayed projects.
Explicitly stating "IFRS, Ind AS and US GAAP are the same"

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION P: Red Flags vs Green Flags (Visual Indicator Matrix)
tableData: Surge in unbilled revenue or contract assets decoupled from cash collection
High risk of future write-offs. Focus valuation strictly on cash generation.
Aggressive upfront recognition of multi-year software/service contracts
Earnings are front-loaded. Rebuild historical margins to a straight-line basis for comparability.
Frequent modifications to contract terms causing revenue spikes
Sign of managing earnings through contract clauses rather than operations.
Transparent disaggregation of revenue by 'point in time' vs 'over time'
Use to segment high-quality recurring revenue from transactional revenue.
Deferred revenue (contract liability) growing consistently with orderbook
Strong indicator of future cash flow visibility. Assign higher valuation multiple.
